data.title: Earnings Scoreboard: 82% of S&P 500 reporting firms top EPS estimates as 78% of firms post Y/Y earnings growth
data.description:
S&P 500 earnings accelerated this week, with over 178 companies from financial, healthcare, and industrial sectors reporting. Key companies included the "Magnificent Seven" giants such as Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META), and Microsoft (MSFT). Results from Coca-Cola (KO), Starbucks (SBUX), Eli Lilly (LLY), and Merck (MRK) helped assess corporate margins amid changing economic conditions.

Earnings Roundup:

Bottom Line: Out of the 178 companies reporting, 147 exceeded EPS expectations, 19 fell short, and 12 met estimates. Notably, 139 firms posted year-over-year earnings growth.

Top Line: Performance remained robust on the revenue front, with 143 companies topping estimates while 35 missed. On a year-over-year basis, 157 of the companies reported revenue expansion.

 

 

Let’s take a look at some of the companies that reported earnings this week:

Microsoft (MSFT): Reported revenue of $82.89B and an EPS of $4.27, both beating analyst estimates, while the company maintained its upbeat cloud growth guidance.

Alphabet (GOOG): Revenue reached $109.9B with an EPS of $5.11, beating expectations on both metrics and prompting the board to maintain its positive AI-driven outlook.

Meta Platforms (META): Posted revenue of $56.31B and an EPS of $10.44, surpassing consensus estimates, though the stock faced pressure after management raised its capital expenditure guidance for the year.

Apple (AAPL): Q2 revenue came in at $111.18B with an EPS of $2.01, beating estimates. iPhone revenue was $56.99B, marginally above estimates, and overall product revenue reached $80.21B, also slightly exceeding forecasts. Mac revenue was $8.4B, up 5.7% year-over-year, surpassing expectations as well.

Amazon (AMZN): Q1 reported revenue of $181.52B and an EPS of $2.78, both significant beats driven by AWS acceleration. It anticipates Q2 revenue between $194.0B and $199.0B, surpassing the $189.5B consensus. It expects operating income of $20.0B to $24.0B, below the $22.9B consensus, assuming Prime Day occurs in Q2.

Starbucks (SBUX): Q2 beats on both revenue ($9.53B) and EPS ($0.50), raises full-year 2026 guidance for comparable store sales and earnings.

Ford (F): Beat EPS estimates at $0.66 on revenue of $39.82B. Buoyed by this momentum, the automaker raised its full-year adjusted EBIT guidance to a range of $8.5 billion to $10.5 billion.

Verizon (VZ): delivered a mixed first-quarter performance, posting a non-GAAP EPS of $1.28 that topped analyst estimates by $0.07. While revenue grew 2.7% year-over-year to $34.4 billion, it fell short of expectations by $490 million. It raised its 2026 adjusted EPS guidance to a growth range of 5.0% to 6.0%, signaling that total retail postpaid phone net additions are now expected to land in the upper half of its previously stated target of 750,000 to one million.

AbbVie (ABBV): delivered a strong top-line performance for the first quarter, with revenue climbing 12.4% year-over-year to $15 billion, surpassing analyst estimates. Non-GAAP EPS of $2.65 narrowly missed expectations. Despite the slight earnings miss, the company raised its full-year 2026 adjusted diluted EPS guidance to a range of $14.08–$14.28.

Caterpillar (CAT): surpasses Q1 earnings and revenue expectations and raises its full-year revenue forecast, driven by demand for power generation equipment.

Exxon Mobil (XOM): after exceeding Q1 adjusted earnings expectations. However, unadjusted net profit fell to a five-year low due to disrupted shipments from the Middle East war and negative timing effects from financial derivatives. Revenue was $85.14B, missing estimates by $150M. 

Visa (V): reported strong fiscal Q2 earnings that surpassed Wall Street predictions. The company reported a fiscal Q2 2026 adjusted EPS of $3.31, exceeding the analyst estimate, and showed net revenue of $11.2B, which also beat the consensus. Visa’s board approved a new $20B stock buyback program. For FY2026, Visa expects EPS growth in the low teens and net revenue growth in the low double digits.

Earnings next week

Next week marks a critical stretch for the S&P 500 as about 120 companies report first-quarter earnings, offering a comprehensive look at the state of the American consumer and healthcare sectors. Major reports are expected from CVS Health (CVS), Pfizer (PFE), Kraft Heinz (KHC), and Marriott (MAR).

Beyond these consumer and health giants, the tech sector will be in focus with highly anticipated results from Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Palantir (PLTR), and Super Micro Computer (SMCI).

Financial and payment platforms such as PayPal (PYPL), Coinbase (COIN), and KKR (KKR) are also on the docket, alongside industrial and energy leaders like Eaton (ETN), Cummins (CMI), and Occidental Petroleum (OXY).

More on markets:

data.title: Insider trades: Abbott, UnitedHealth and ServiceNow among notable names
data.description:
Daniel J. Starks, a director at Abbott Laboratories (NYSE: ABT), acquired 10,000 shares of the company’s common stock on April 27, 2026, for a total value of $926,537, bringing his total shares  holding to 6.75M. Separately, EVP and CFO Philip P. Boudreau disclosed several share-related transactions dated April 23, 2026, including an open-market purchase of 2,200 shares at $91.50 each, held indirectly through his spouse. Separately, Boudreau reports 71,172 common shares held directly after these transactions. The purchases come at a notable time, with Abbott stock trading near its 52-week low of $, reflecting a decline of roughly 29% so far this year.

S&P Global (SPGI) CEO Martina Cheung purchased 2,322 shares of the company’s stock on Wednesday, April 29, at an average price of $429.93 per share, amounting to a total investment of $998,297.46. Following the transaction, Cheung’s holdings increased to 27,518 shares, valued at approximately $11.83 million, marking a 9.22% rise in her ownership stake.

Scott Tozier, a director at International Paper (IP), acquired 10,000 shares of the company on May 1, 2026, for an estimated $313,009. Following the transaction, he owns a total of 10,025 shares of International Paper.

President and CEO Christopher L. Winfrey purchased a total of 6,936 Charter (CHTR) Class A shares through open-market transactions on April 28, 2026, including a separate entry for 3,468 shares held by his spouse, at a weighted average price of about $172, lifting his direct stake to 74,409 shares. In a separate filing, director Wade Davis acquired 5,728 shares on April 28, 2026, at $173.72 per share. Additionally, Nair Balan purchased 1,000 shares at $175.46, bringing his total stake to 12,029 shares.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) executive Patrick Hugh Conway, CEO of Optum, disclosed an open-market sale of 800 shares of the company’s common stock at $355.00 per share. Following the transaction, he directly owns 17,804.991 shares.

Jacqueline P. Canney, Chief People & AI Enablement Officer of ServiceNow (NOW), sold 8,927 shares of common stock on April 24, 2026, at $89.601 per share, for a total of $799,868. Following the transaction, she directly held 29,531 shares of the company’s common stock.

Halliburton (HAL) director Tobi M. Young disclosed sales of common stock and holdings in restricted stock units. On April 30, 2026, Young sold a total of 6,125 shares in open-market transactions at prices of $41.72 and $41.725 per share.

data.title: Berkshire earnings rebound in Q1 thanks to insurance-underwriting
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) announced its Q1 2026 financials on Saturday, indicating a rise in its operating earnings following a sharp drop in the preceding quarter as its insurance-underwriting business offset a decline in insurance-investment income.

In Q4 2025, Berkshire (BRK.B) posted $10.2B in operating earnings with a ~30% drop as after-tax earnings from its insurance underwriting and insurance investment income fell ~54% YoY and 25% YoY, respectively.

However, the Omaha, Nebraska-based company reported $11.3B in operating earnings with ~18% YoY growth for the latest quarter, which was the first under Greg Abel, who succeeded billionaire investor Warren Buffett as CEO in January.

The rise in operating earnings reflected Berkshire’s (BRK.B) Insurance—underwriting business, which added $1.7B with a ~29% YoY growth, thanks mainly to a lack of significant catastrophe events during the quarter.

However, its insurance-investment income slipped ~7% YoY to $2.7B, primarily due to a decline in interest income attributed to lower interest rates.

Its railroad business, BNSF, which runs one of the largest railroad systems in North America, added $1.4B with a ~13% YoY rise driven by higher revenues and operational efficiencies. Despite a mixed performance, Berkshire's (BRK.B) manufacturing, service, and retailing operations added $3.2B with ~5% YoY growth.

Berkshire Hathaway Energy Company generated $1.1B with a ~2% YoY rise, benefiting from higher earnings from the natural gas pipeline businesses and federal income tax credits, which offset lower earnings from U.S. utilities and other energy units.

The conglomerate's financial position continued to strengthen as its cash, cash equivalents, and short-term securities stood at $397.4B as of March 31, implying a ~6% YoY growth amid nearly $8.1B in sales of equity securities on a net basis.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway Q1 operating earnings +18% Y/Y, cash hits $397.4B
data.description:
• Berkshire Hathaway press release (BRK.A) (BRK.B): Q1 operating earnings of $11.3B (+17.7% Y/Y).
  • Net earnings per average equivalent Class A share of $7,027
  • Net earnings per average equivalent Class B share of $4.68
  • Cash, cash equivalents, and short-term securities of $397.4B
  • SEC filing

data.title: Trending stocks this week as Big Tech earnings lift markets amid Fed pause
data.description:
The stock market ended the week higher as investors worked through a wave of strong Big Tech earnings, while also navigating a divided Federal Reserve stance and renewed uncertainty in global oil markets.

The Federal Reserve held its policy rate steady at 3.50%–3.75% for a third consecutive meeting, citing an uncertain economic outlook. Geopolitical tensions also intensified, with President Donald Trump reportedly rejecting Iran’s proposal to lift a naval blockade ahead of nuclear negotiations.

In energy markets, the United Arab Emirates said it will exit OPEC and OPEC+, pointing to a strategic shift aimed at gaining more flexibility over its oil production policy.

On the earnings front, Apple (AAPL) beat expectations on strong iPhone 17 demand, while Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG), and Microsoft (MSFT) reinforced that heavy AI spending is driving returns.

Here's what caught investor attention this week:

Meta (META) dropped 10% following the release of its quarterly report, amid lackluster guidance and an increase in its already aggressive capital expenditure budget. Largely driven by AI investment, the Facebook parent said it sees $125B -$145B in capital expenditures in 2026, up from its prior target of $115B-$135B.

Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) jumped after strong results and signaling even larger AI-driven spending ahead, raising its 2026 CapEx outlook to $180B–$190B and flagging further increases in 2027. The company delivered a strong Q1 beat, with revenue rising 22% to $109.9B and net income surging 81%, boosted by gains on nonmarketable equity securities that more than tripled vs. a year ago.

PayPal (PYPL) recently announced a major corporate reorganization. The firm split its operations into three distinct business units, with one centered on Consumer Financial Services & Venmo. The move effectively set up Venmo as a stand-alone operating segment within the company.

Amazon (AMZN) released its first-quarter earnings report, with total revenue up 16.6% year-over-year to $181.5B, vs. $177.2B consensus. The cloud business was strong again as AWS revenue came in at $37.6B (+28% Y/Y) to beat the consensus estimate of $36.7B. Free cash flow decreased to $1.2B for the trailing twelve months, driven primarily by a year-over-year increase of $59.3B in purchases of property and equipment, net of proceeds from sales and incentives. Of note, the increase primarily reflects investments in artificial intelligence by Amazon (AMZN).

Qualcomm (QCOM) on Wednesday reported Q2 results largely in line with expectations, while its Q3 guidance missed estimates, but shares surged on optimism around new data center opportunities. Q2 non-GAAP EPS came in at $2.65, beating estimates; revenue declined 3.6% to $10.59B, roughly in line. It was Qualcomm's expansion into data centers that turned investor attention as it said it secured a "leading hyperscaler" as a customer, shipping its custom chips from the December quarter.

Apple (AAPL) on Thursday reported strong second-quarter earnings driven by strong demand for the iPhone 17. Per-share profit was $2.01, beating estimates by $0.07, on revenue of $111.18B, which grew 16.5%, also ahead of expectations. CEO Tim Cook flagged rising memory prices, which are expected to weigh more heavily on the company’s costs in coming quarters. Speaking on the company’s earnings call on Thursday, Cook said memory costs are set to be "significantly higher" in the June quarter and will have a larger effect beyond that.

The Pentagon entered into agreements with SpaceX (SPACE), OpenAI (OPENAI), Google (GOOG), Nvidia (NVDA), Reflection AI, Microsoft (MSFT), and Amazon Web Services (AMZN) to deploy their advanced AI capabilities on classified networks. “These agreements accelerate the transformation toward establishing the United States military as an AI-first fighting force and will strengthen our warfighters’ ability to maintain decision superiority across all domains of warfare,” according to a statement.

SanDisk (SNDK) surged after delivering a blowout fiscal Q3, with revenue jumping 251% year over year to $5.95B, well above estimates, while EPS of $23.41 crushed expectations of $14.66. Gross margins of 78.4% also came in sharply ahead of forecasts, underscoring strong pricing power in the memory cycle.

Roblox (RBLX) shares fell after the gaming platform’s first-quarter user metrics missed estimates. Daily active users grew by nearly 34M to 132M but were less than the 143.8M estimate, while hours engaged were up 43% to 31B, shy of 33.7B estimates. Looking ahead to the current quarter, Roblox (RBLX) expects revenue to be within a range of $1.39B and $1.45B, well below $1.90B estimates. For FY26, Roblox (RBLX) lowered its revenue guidance to reflect the continuation of the safety headwinds, now expected to increase by 20% to 25% to within a range of $5.878B and $6.14B versus previous guidance for growth of 23% to 29%.

The Coca-Cola Company (KO) topped estimates with its first-quarter earnings report. Revenue was up 12.6% year-over-year to $12.5B for the quarter that ended on March 31. The revenue performance was driven by an 8% increase in concentrate sales and 2% growth in price/mix. On the guidance front, Coca-Cola (KO) still sees full-year organic sales growth of about 4% to 5% (midpoint 4.5%) vs. 4.8% consensus. EPS growth of 7% to 8% is anticipated.

Celestica (CLS) shares tumbled on Monday even as the Canadian technology firm reported first-quarter results and guidance that topped estimates. For the period ending March 31, Celestica said it earned an adjusted $2.16 per share as revenue rose 52.8% year-over-year to come in at $4.05B. Looking to the second-quarter, Celestica expects to earn between $2.14 and $2.34 per share on an adjusted basis. Sales are forecast to be between $4.15B and $4.45B.

Organon (OGN) shares surged after Sun Pharmaceutical Industries agreed to acquire the company in an all-cash deal valuing it at ~$11.75B enterprise value, with shareholders set to receive $14 per share. The transaction, expected to close in early 2027, strengthens Sun Pharma’s global footprint and expands its presence in women’s health, biosimilars, and developed markets, while creating a combined entity with ~$12.4B in revenue.

SoFi Technologies (SOFI) fell after posting in-line Q1 results, with EPS meeting estimates and revenue slightly beating. The company reaffirmed its 2026 outlook, offering no upside catalyst for investors.

data.title: Spirit Airlines shutting down as bailout talks fall apart
data.description:
Spirit Aviation Holdings (FLYYQ), the parent company of Spirit Airlines, LLC, announced on Saturday that the ultralow-cost airline has started to wind down its operations, effective immediately, after failing to secure financial support amid soaring oil prices.

The announcement came after reports indicated on Friday that certain bondholders of the company and the government failed to agree on a $500M lifeline offered by the Trump administration to save the struggling airline.

“All Spirit flights have been cancelled, and Spirit guests should not go to the airport,” the company, which once accounted for nearly 5% of U.S. flights with its bright yellow planes, said in a statement.

Spirit (FLYYQ) added that it will automatically start processing refunds for any flights booked using credit or debit cards. In the case of flights booked through travel agents, customers should contact the agents to request refunds. Compensation for bookings made using any other method, such as Free Spirit points, will be determined through the bankruptcy process, according to the statement.

"In March 2026, we reached an agreement with our bondholders on a restructuring plan that would have allowed us to emerge as a go-forward business,” Spirit (FLYYQ) CEO Dave Davis said. “However, the sudden and sustained rise in fuel prices in recent weeks ultimately has left us with no alternative but to pursue an orderly wind-down of the Company,” he added.

Spirit’s (FLYYQ) exit is expected to benefit its competitors, such as Frontier Airlines (ULCC) and JetBlue Airways (JBLU), whose shares closed higher on Friday after The Wall Street Journal reported that discussions over the government bailout were falling apart.

data.title: Frank stuff: Costco's $1.50 hot dog deal might be unmatched in all of retail
data.description:
Costco Wholesale (COST) announced this week that it is now offering customers the option to order a 16.9-ounce Kirkland bottled water instead of a 20-ounce soda with its iconic $1.50 hot dog combo. While the menu change is rather minor, it is noteworthy because it is the only revision to the $1.50 hot dog deal in more than 40 years. Social media reaction to the change has been positive, with many Costco (COST) customers noting that they do not drink soda. 

Due to inflation, the $1.50 hot dog cuts into Costco's (NASDAQ:COST) profits on a nominal basis but is widely considered an overall driver of store traffic and membership loyalty. "The hotdog became a psychological anchor, a reminder to customers that Costco is built on trust, fairness, and value that feels untouchable. Even though the company loses close to $300M annually on the deal, it wins far more in membership renewals, loyalty, and priceless brand equity," highlighted Shopper AI brand strategist Alon Barak.

The history of the $1.50 hot dog at Costco (COSTCO) tracks to the introduction at the food court in 1984, shortly after the company’s 1983 founding. In a short time, the hot dog and soda combo quickly became a defining feature of the Costco (COST) shopping experience. As membership and food courts expanded, Costco (COST) transitioned to manufacturing its own Kirkland-branded hot dogs. The company also opened facilities in California and Illinois to reduce supply chain costs and preserve the low price.

Costco (COST) management has long been vocal about the significance of this offering. Within the last year, CFO Gary Millerchip and CEO Ron Vachris have publicly declared their commitment to maintaining the $1.50 price.

Is there a similar pricing sensation in all of retail? The closest parallel might be AriZona Iced Tea's $0.99 price point for a can of iced tea that has been held steady since 1992. The $0.99 is printed on the can and generated strong brand loyalty, although the family-owned company does not control how individual stores sell the product, so the price can vary.

For its part, Walmart's (WMT) Sam's Club has countered the Costco (COST) hot dog deal with a price of $1.38 and a larger 20-ounce soda size. One key difference is that generally anyone can walk into a Sam's Club food court, while Costco (COST) checks for membership status.

If you can think of a better deal in all of retail, add it to the comment stream.

data.title: 5 out of 5 communications services stocks deliver EPS wins this week: Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
Communications services stocks grabbed the spotlight this week as Wall Street digested earnings reports from major names including Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Meta Platforms (META).

The communications services sector (XLC) lost 0.8% so far this year, compared to the over 5% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

In this week's earnings recap, five tickers reported results, out of which all reported earnings beat. On the revenue side, four companies exceeded revenue expectations, while one company trailed estimates.

Below are the latest quarterly reports from some of the key players, which reported results this week:

Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) topped expectations in a first-quarter earnings report, with total revenues in the first quarter rising 22% marking its 11th straight quarter of double-digit growth. However, the bigger point of discussion was capex guidance. The company raised its full-year 2026 capital expenditure outlook to $180B–$190B, up from $175B–$185B, and went a step further, flagging that 2027 CapEx will increase significantly over 2026 as it ramps investments to meet what it called “unprecedented” demand for AI compute.

Meta Platforms (META) beat on both the top- and bottom-line in the first quarter, but issued lackluster forward guidance. Like Alphabet, Meta also raised its capex expectations by a significant amount, anticipating higher component pricing and increased data center costs. Meta stock closed over 8% lower on Thursday.

Telecom giant Verizon’s (VZ) first-quarter results topped profit expectations, with the company delivering a milestone in wireless subscriber growth.  Rival T-Mobile (TMUS) also posted first-quarter earnings where it beat expectations on top and bottom lines and boosted full-year subscriber growth guidance.

For the upcoming week, Walt Disney (DIS), Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), Paramount Skydance (PSKY) and Electronic Arts (EA) are some communication services names that are scheduled to report quarterly results.

data.title: 8 out of 10 S&P 500 utility stocks beat EPS estimates this week: Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
Eight out of the 10 S&P 500 utilities companies that reported their quarterly results this week surpassed earnings expectations. On the revenue front too, eight out of the 10 companies beat Wall Street forecasts.

The State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) rose 9% year-to-date, compared to the S&P 500’s (SP500) 5.6% gain.

Below are the latest quarterly reports from five industry giants:

FirstEnergy (FE) outlined $2.62-$2.82 2026 core EPS guidance while reaffirming 6%-8% CAGR through 2030. CEO Brian Tierney said the company “is off to a solid start this year with first quarter core earnings 7.5% above last year.”

Entergy (ETR) targets a $57B 4-year capital plan as it targets 8.5% retail sales CAGR through 2029. The company expects the adjusted EPS outlook for next year to be $0.20 higher, and "the increase grows ratably from $0.50 in 2029 to $6.40".

CMS Energy (CMS) CEO Garrick Rochow meanwhile framed the quarter around regulatory support, load growth, and reaffirmed financial targets, saying, "Our investment thesis... continues to stand the test of time" and reiterating the company’s TSR formula as "6% to 8% adjusted EPS growth with annual compounding, paired with approximately 3% dividend yield."

Southern (SO) outlined a $1.00 adjusted EPS estimate for Q2 2026 amid $26.5B DOE loan agreements, and beat estimates in Q1. First-quarter 2026 operating revenues were $8.4 billion, compared with $7.8 billion for the first quarter of 2025, an increase of 8%.

Edison (EIX) reported a mixed quarterly print but keeps confidence in delivering 5-7% core EPS growth from 2025-2030. Expects SCE rate base compound annual growth of approximately 7% from 2025 to 2030.

Utilities ETFs: (XLU), (VPU), (FUTY), (IDU), (FXU), and (JXI)

data.title: Liberty Global outlines EUR 500M 2028 free cash flow path for Ziggo Group while reconfirming 2026 guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Liberty Global (LBTYA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered strong operational performance" and "this was our fourth straight quarter of steady broadband improvement across each of our big 3 markets with fixed and mobile ARPUs remaining largely stable," said Michael Fries (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board).
  • "We will be confirming all of our 2026 guidance today," Fries said, adding that the company is progressing on value-unlock steps: "The acquisition of Vodafone's 50% stake in our Dutch JV is on track to close this summer" and the Benelux spin plan remains "in the second half of next year" for the Ziggo Group.
  • "After funding the $1.2 billion needed to close the Vodafone transaction and executing on around $700 million of asset sales from our growth portfolio, we should end the year with around $1.5 billion of corporate cash," Fries said, also noting "through April, we've generated around $300 million in proceeds."
  • "VodafoneZiggo reported a revenue decline of 1.8% in Q1" and "adjusted EBITDA declined 6.4%," said Charles Bracken (Executive VP & CFO).
  • "Virgin Media O2 delivered a total service revenue decline of 3%" and "adjusted EBITDA declined by 3.4%" (including "a noncash provision for legal matters"), Bracken said.

Outlook

  • "We are reconfirming all guidance metrics of VMO2, VodafoneZiggo and Telenet as well as our guidance for corporate costs," Bracken said.
  • "We are aiming to end 2026 with around $1.5 billion of corporate cash despite the expected outflows associated with the incremental Vodafone stake and also, to a lesser extent, the Netomnia acquisition," Bracken said.
  • Prior-quarter context: Liberty Global previously guided VMO2 "total service revenues" to "decline by 3% to 5%" and adjusted EBITDA to "decline by 3% to 5%," and in Q1 management stated it is "confirming all of our 2026 guidance" (no updated numeric ranges were stated in the Q1 transcript).

Financial Results

  • "We ended the quarter with a consolidated cash balance of $1.9 billion," Bracken said.
  • "The fair market value of our growth portfolio remained broadly stable versus 2025 year-end at $3.4 billion," Bracken said.
  • "Telenet reported EUR 10 million of free cash flow during the quarter and is expected to deliver at least EUR 20 million of free cash flow for the full year," Bracken said.
  • "Liberty Corporate delivered adjusted EBITDA of negative $2 million, putting us firmly on track to achieve our full year 2026 guidance of negative $50 million," Bracken said.

Q&A

  • Carl Murdock-Smith, Citigroup: asked if VMO2’s "GBP 15 million of fixed pre-enablement and installation income" reflected an accounting change and whether it affected the "3% to 5% decline" service revenue guide; also asked about O2 Satellite. Lutz Schuler (CEO) said "you're right" it "was not -- we didn't account it the same way before" and added "it won't change anything in the guidance."
  • Polo Tang, UBS: asked about U.K. price rises, churn, and mobile stabilization; also asked if Liberty is "pivoting" toward media/sports vs. break-up and shareholder returns. Fries said "our primary commitment... is to create value for shareholders" and "we are committed to the unlock strategy." Schuler said after the price rise "we don't see any spike in churn" and "we are planning for a recovery in mobile service revenue."
  • Robert Grindle, Deutsche Bank: asked about timing risk in Belgium regulator approval and whether Wyre/Telenet financials could change with wholesale rates. An unnamed executive said regulators are "right up to date" and management is "cautiously optimistic" for completion "over the next... 6 to 8 weeks." Bracken said "we'll get a more stable view on the numbers in Q2" due to "movement on the wholesale rate to Telenet" and "management fees" being reevaluated.
  • Joshua Mills, BNP Paribas: asked about Ziggo Group leverage path and asset-sale components; also asked drivers of Dutch broadband improvement and any benefit from Odido breach. Fries said asset sales are "primarily of towers and technical facilities" but "we're not really providing a breakdown... because we're in active sale process." Stephen van Rooyen (CEO) said improvements came from fixing pricing, lowering churn, and marketing/speed upgrades, while Odido impact was "nothing that I think is material in the Q1 results."
  • James Ratzer, New Street: asked what drove Telenet broadband net adds and Wyre revenue decline dynamics. An unnamed executive said BASE growth is "about 50-50 between the Telenet footprint and growth in the South" and Wyre decline reflected "a new wholesale deal on the HFC" with "an inflationary component" going forward.
  • Matthew Harrigan, Benchmark: asked about sovereignty/defense tailwinds. Fries said the sovereignty focus is "net positive for us in the telecom space" but "we are not involved in any specific defense type investment opportunities."
  • Ulrich Rathe, Bernstein: asked about regulatory climate and Ziggo synergies. Fries said new EU merger guidelines are "quite positive" and synergies include "a financial synergy" plus "operating costs" and "procurement and CapEx."
  • David Wright, BofA: asked about DOCSIS 4.0 timing and InfraCo/ServCo agility. Fries said "4- and 8-gig trials are the latter part of the year" and the ServCo model in Belgium "forces you to be more efficient."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were neutral to slightly negative, with repeated probing on accounting-driven revenue, competitive churn, leverage path, regulatory timing, and disclosure limits (e.g., "could you maybe talk through... churn" and "can you just remind us... pro forma leverage").
  • Management was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded on specifics in Q&A, using confidence language on approvals and deal paths (e.g., Fries: "we're pretty confident this deal will be approved"; unnamed executive: "cautiously optimistic") while declining to quantify certain items (e.g., Fries: "we're not really providing a breakdown" of asset-sale components).
  • Versus the prior quarter, the tone shifted from announcing major transactions (Vodafone stake and Netomnia) to execution and regulatory/process updates, with more Q&A focused on accounting, churn sensitivity, and mechanics of separation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 emphasized transaction announcements and detailed numeric 2026 OpCo guidance ranges; Q1 emphasized execution, stating "we will be confirming all of our 2026 guidance" without adding new numeric ranges.
  • Q1 added operational proof-points (e.g., Fries: "best in 3 years" for Dutch broadband) and process milestones (Vodafone stake "on track to close this summer"; Netomnia "now officially in the regulatory process").
  • Analyst questioning moved from deal structure and commitments (Q4) toward revenue recognition, churn after price rises, and regulator timing/wholesale-rate impacts (Q1).

Risks and Concerns

  • "The noise from 1 or 2 competitors has escalated recently" around the Netomnia process, but Fries said "we're pretty confident this deal will be approved."
  • Bracken cited operating pressure drivers including "higher marketing costs," "incremental investments in network resilience and service reliability," "competitive pressure" in U.K. consumer fixed, and "intense competition" in Ireland.
  • Regulatory timing remained a focus for Belgium fiber cooperation; management said a prolonged delay beyond the stated window was unlikely (unnamed executive: "It's a virtual impossibility that it would go longer than that").

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as an execution quarter: Liberty Global reconfirmed 2026 guidance, highlighted improving broadband trends and largely stable ARPUs across key markets, and reiterated timelines for major value-unlock steps, including closing Vodafone’s Dutch JV stake acquisition in the summer and progressing regulatory review for the Netomnia transaction. The company also emphasized capital allocation and liquidity, including $1.9 billion consolidated cash at quarter-end and an objective to end 2026 with around $1.5 billion of corporate cash after planned transaction outflows and growth-portfolio asset sales.
